title: Hooked rug landscape panel
subtitle: A pictorial hooked rug with a simple countryside landscape in cut wool strips
excerpt: Hook a pictorial wall panel showing a simple landscape with sky, hills, and foreground field using cut wool strips on hessian backing. The panel uses shading within each area to suggest depth. The finished piece is about 40 cm by 30 cm and is designed to hang rather than be walked on.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional hooked-rug practice. Pictorial landscape rug hooking is documented in the British rug-hooking tradition, particularly the Northern England and Scottish pictorial tradition from the 19th and 20th centuries.

glossaryTerms.definition: A textile technique in which cut strips of fabric or yarn are pulled up through a woven backing (hessian or monks cloth) using a hooked tool to form a looped pile surface.
A narrow strip of wool fabric or yarn cut to a consistent width before hooking. The strip width affects the pile height and texture of the finished rug.

Placing lighter and darker tones of a colour in adjacent rows within one area to suggest form or depth. In landscape hooked rugs, shading the sky from pale at the horizon to deeper blue overhead creates a convincing perspective.

content.text: within each colour zone to give the flat
a sense of depth. Simple landscapes work best: three or four horizontal zones (sky, distant hills, mid-ground, foreground) each in a 3 to 4-value colour range.

items.name: Hessian or monks cloth backing, 50 by 40 cm
Wool fabric cut into 6 mm strips in sky colours (3 to 4 blues and whites)
Wool fabric in hill and field colours (greens, olive, gold)
Woollen binding tape for the edge

content.text: Step 1: Transfer the design to the backing'

Draw the main zones onto the hessian with a marker: a horizon line about one-third up from the base, a gentle hill outline above it, and a flat strip at the front. Keep the shapes simple; small detail is hard to hook clearly.

Work from the top of the panel downward. Use the palest blue or white near the horizon line and deeper sky blue toward the top of the panel. Hook in curving horizontal rows that follow the shape of the sky area. Introduce a second slightly darker blue in some rows and a white for cloud shapes worked in short curved sections.
Work the hill with the palest grey-green at the far distance and warmer, richer greens as the hills come closer. Hook in lines that follow the hill shape, not in flat horizontal rows.
Hook the foreground with gold and olive strips. Work dark at the base and light near the hills. Dark warm tones look close; pale cool tones look far.

Trim any loops that are longer than the surrounding pile. Bind the edges of the hessian backing with woollen binding tape, folding it over the raw edge and stitching it in place on the back. Mount for hanging by sewing a hanging sleeve of fabric to the back.

items.problem: The landscape zones look like flat stripes with no sense of depth
items.cause: Each zone is one flat colour with no tonal variation within it
items.fix: Introduce at least three tones within each zone: a lighter highlight area, a mid-value for most of the zone, and a slightly darker shadow or far-distance tone. Even a small range of three close values creates a stronger sense of space than one flat colour.

items.problem: Loops are pulling out when the panel is handled
items.cause: The strip was not pulled up far enough to clear the warp thread on the hessian before moving to the next hole
items.fix: Each loop should stand about 6 mm above the backing surface. If loops are lower they sit below the warp thread level and pull out easily. Re-hook any loose areas with slightly higher loops.
